items.passage: Every living thing carries instructions inside its cells that decide how it will grow. These instructions are stored in long molecules called genes, which parents pass down to their children. Most children look a bit like their parents because they share many of the same genes. Sometimes, however, small changes appear, and these changes help explain why each person is unique.

Wind power is one of the fastest growing clean energy sources in the world. Tall turbines stand in open fields and along coasts, where the wind blows steadily for most of the day. Their blades turn slowly when the wind is light and faster when storms come in. The energy they make is sent through cables to homes and small towns nearby.

Memory is the way our brains keep useful pieces of past events ready for later. Some memories last for only a few seconds, like a phone number you say once and forget. Other memories stay with us for years, such as the first day of school or a family trip. Sleep seems to help the brain decide which of these memories to keep.

Ancient Egypt grew along the banks of the Nile, a river that flooded each summer and left rich soil behind. Farmers planted wheat and barley as soon as the water pulled back, and they fed cities along the river. Builders used the same river to move heavy stones from far quarries to the famous pyramids. Without the Nile, the country would have stayed a hot desert with little food.

Volcanoes form where hot rock from deep inside the earth pushes up through cracks in the surface. When the pressure becomes too high, the rock breaks free and rushes out as glowing lava. Ash and small stones may also fall on towns nearby. After many quiet years, the cooled lava builds a tall cone that we can see for miles around.

Every planet in our solar system has its own kind of air, and these layers shape what each world looks like. Mars has a thin, cold blanket of carbon dioxide that lets sunlight reach the dusty ground. Venus is wrapped in thick clouds that trap heat and keep the surface hotter than an oven. Saturn and Jupiter, on the other hand, are mostly gas, with no clear surface to land on.
